  Chandrayaan-3 in 2023.                                      increasing the gap between urban and rural India.
y Poverty rates have fallen dramatically: rural poverty     y Rapid industrialisation has worsened environmental issues—
  dropped from 25.7% in 2012 to 4.8% in 2023-24,              New Delhi remains one of the most polluted cities due to
  and urban poverty to around 4%.                             unchecked growth.

10. Effect of Globalization on Tribals
                      Positive Impact                                                   Negative Impact
y Economic opportunities and income generation through              y The exploitation of natural resources in tribal areas
  industries, tourism, and global markets, helping preserve           causes environmental damage and harms tribal
  cultural heritage→ Araku Valley Tribal Coffee initiative in         livelihoods.
  Andhra Pradesh, supported by international markets and            y Many GI benefits do not reach the actual tribal
  organisations, empowers tribal farmers.                             producers but instead go to traders or middlemen.
y Some tribal products have received Geographical Indication        y Commercialisation often leads to development-induced
  (GI) tags, which have increased their value and market              displacement & exploitation, causing structural
  price→ Price of Kadaknath Chicken (GI tag) raised incomes           alienation of tribals from ‘Jal-Jungle-Jameen’.
  for Bhil tribals.                                                 y Traditional knowledge, languages, and rituals are being
y Commercialisation of tribal handicrafts and artefacts has           lost as young tribal generations migrate to cities or
  created new income sources, and employment→ Pithora                 adopt non-tribal lifestyles.
  paintings by Bhil and Rathwa tribes are now sold in wider         y Limited employment opportunities in industrial
  markets, helping some artists earn a livelihood.                    projects within tribal regions often exclude local tribal
y Improved access to education and healthcare, empowering             populations.
  tribal communities and enhancing their well-being.                y Economic policies favouring external investors often
y Greater access to information and technology, connecting            neglect tribal rights and welfare, leading to social and
  tribals to global resources and markets.                            economic disparities→ In Jharkhand, coal mining has
y Preservation and international recognition of tribal culture,       displaced Santhal and Ho tribes without adequate
  traditions, festivals, and arts, fostering cultural pride           rehabilitation.

11. Effect of Globalization on Social Movements
   Feature                     Traditional Movements                              Globalization-Era Movements
Geographical        National / Local: Confined within state borders;        Transnational: Operates as "Global Coalitions"
scope               focused on domestic policy change.                      across borders (e.g., Fridays for Future).
Focus               Class & Economy: Centered on labor rights,              Identity & Quality of Life: Focuses on New
                    wages, and material distribution.                       Social Movements with issues like feminism,
                                                                            LGBTQ+, and climate justice.
Structure           Hierarchical: Led by defined leaders or                 Networked / Leaderless: Decentralized structures
                    organizations (unions/parties) with central             often form “loosely coordinated networks” with
                    command.                                                informal leadership
Primary Tool        Physical Presence: Relied on strikes, street rallies,   Digital Mobilization: Uses social media as an
                    and physical pamphlets to spread the word.              initiator for rapid, real-time global coordination.


Main Target            The Nation-State: Addressed grievances to the         Global Entities: Targets MNCs and international
                       central government for legislative reform.            bodies (WTO/World Bank) for systemic change.
Identity               Homogeneous: Usually comprises a single class         Heterogeneous: "Rainbow coalitions" of students,
                       or group (e.g., factory workers).                     indigenous tribes, and diverse urban professionals.

12. Challenges due to Globalisation
      Challenge                                                          Description
Political Crises and           Rise of populism, protectionism, and geopolitical tensions (e.g., Brexit) as a backlash against
Global Conflicts               Globalisation, threatening political stability & global cooperation.
Wealth Concentration           The top 10% control the majority of resources, exacerbating disparities within and between
& Inequality                   nations→ Global North-Global South divide.
Economic                       Contagion Effect: Localized shocks (e.g., 2008 Crisis, pandemic supply chain breaks) trigger
Interdependence                global financial instability.
Political Crises &             The rise of populism, protectionism, and geopolitical fragmentation as a backlash against
Sovereign Friction             unfettered globalisation. The hyper-influence of transnational corporations challenges the policy
                               autonomy of nation-states, driving intense nativist friction. Examples: Brexit; transitioning from
                               NAFTA to the USMCA to protect domestic markets.
Ghettoization of               Globalised supply chains systematically segregate women workers into the lowest-paid, most
Female Labor                   precarious informal tiers of production keeping them trapped in low-mobility, home-based, or
                               unorganized sectors with zero social safety nets.
Exploitative Corporate         MNCs engage in "Race to the Bottom" by seeking countries with the weakest labor and
Practices                      environmental laws, leading to sweatshops and poor safety standards.
Crisis Among Local             Developing countries’ farmers face severe challenges as markets are flooded with cheaper
Farmers                        agricultural imports, threatening their livelihoods and economic sustainability→ may also
                               lead to food insecurity.
Platform Capitalism &          Centralisation of digital infrastructure in the hands of global tech monopolies drives Platform
Gig Economy in the             Capitalism, concentrating economic opportunities in dense urban hubs while shifting
Digital Era                    employment from secure, formal contracts to precarious, algorithmic gig labor with zero
                               structural stability.
Cultural                       "McDonaldization" of culture; erosion of indigenous languages/traditions replaced by
Homogenization                 standardized global consumerist values.

13. Deglobalization
                                              Indicators of Deglobalization
y Trade Decline: Weak global demand and rising import barriers have reduced global trade.
y Reduced Capital Flow: Post-financial crisis and regulatory tightening have caused a slump in cross-border bank loans.
y COVID-19 Impact: The pandemic exposed fragile global supply chains, pushing nations toward self-sufficiency,
  especially in health and essentials.
y Withdrawal from Global Agreements: The U.S. exited major multilateral agreements like the Paris Climate Accord and
  the WHO, signalling a retreat from global cooperation and a shift towards economic nationalism.

                                               Reasons for Deglobalization
y Intensifying Geopolitical Conflicts: Ongoing conflicts such as the Russia–Ukraine War and Israel–Gaza conflict have
  disrupted trade flows, triggered sanctions, and heightened global instability.
y Declining Effectiveness of Global Institutions: Institutions like the World Trade Organization, the International
  Monetary Fund, and the World Health Organization face limitations in resolving disputes and managing crises,
  prompting unilateral actions.
y Growing Protectionist Policies Worldwide: Countries increasingly adopt tariffs, subsidies, and localisation strategies
  to protect domestic industries and reduce external dependence.
y Weakening of Regional Economic Cooperation: Major developments like Brexit and the U.S. withdrawal from the
  Trans-Pacific Partnership have undermined multilateral trade frameworks.
y Persistent Global Supply Chain Vulnerabilities: Events such as COVID-19, the Suez Canal blockage, and Red Sea
  disruptions (2024) exposed fragility in global logistics networks.
y Rising Global Economic Inequality: Wealth concentration has intensified, with top income groups controlling
  disproportionate global assets, fuelling discontent against globalisation.


                                                 Impact of Deglobalisation
 y Slower Global Economic Growth: Reduced trade, investment flows, and efficiency losses weaken global growth
   momentum and hinder overall economic expansion.
 y Rising Costs and Inflationary Pressures: Reshoring and trade barriers increase production costs, leading to higher prices
   for goods and services globally.
 y Fragmentation of Global Supply Chains: Shift toward “friend-shoring” and regionalisation disrupts integrated global
   production networks, reducing efficiency and reliability.
 y Reduced Opportunities for Developing Countries: Declining exports, FDI, and outsourcing limit growth prospects,
   employment generation, and poverty reduction in developing economies.
 y Weakening of Multilateral Institutions: Global institutions lose relevance as countries adopt unilateral or regional
   approaches, undermining cooperative global governance.
 y On Indian Society:
      ♦ On Youth: Focus shifting from "Brain Drain" to building domestic tech "Unicorns."
      ♦ On Culture: A "Return to Roots"—revival of millets, handlooms, and regional cinema (e.g., the Kantara or RRR
         effect).
      ♦ Revival of Local Wisdom: Encourages a return to sustainable, local living—Ayurveda, organic farming, and
         traditional water harvesting—which are more resilient to global shocks.

    Deglobalization as an Opportunity for India                                    Risks of Deglobalization
 y Atmanirbhar Bharat (Self-Reliance): It provides a               y Rise of Nativism and "Son of the Soil" Sentiments:
   vacuum for Indian industries to grow without being stifled        Deglobalization often fuels nativism—the preference for
   by cheap global imports→ Production Linked Incentive              "insiders" over "outsiders."
   (PLI) Schemes for domestic manufacturing.                       y Decreased Tolerance: The "inward-looking" shift under
 y Revival of Local Wisdom: It encourages a return to                deglobalization often results in lower acceptance of foreign
   sustainable, local living, which is more resilient to global      ideas or diverse perspectives, potentially eroding the
   shocks→ Focus on traditional water harvesting                     cosmopolitan spirit of India's major cities.
   practices.                                                      y Isolationism: When a society stops engaging with global
 y Demographic Dividend: With a massive young                        perspectives, it prevents the exchange of ideas that drives
   population, India can become its own largest consumer             social reform and progressive thinking & risks becoming an
   market, reducing vulnerability to global economic                 echo chamber.
   recessions.                                                     y Technological Stagnation: Deglobalization restricts the
 y Digital Sovereignty: Developing indigenous tech                   free flow of information, due to which India may face a
   platforms (like UPI) ensures that India’s economic and            technological lag in critical sectors like healthcare, AI, and
   social data remains within its borders.                           green energy.

14. Way Forward
          Aspect                                                       Recommendation
 Cultural Heritage and         Promote "Digital Documentation" and heritage conservation to safeguard linguistic diversity and
 Diversity Preservation        counter cultural homogenization→ Bhashini & Gyan Bharatam: Using AI to translate
                               traditional knowledge and promote Indian languages globally.
 Sustainable Development       Transition to Circular Economy models and green technologies to mitigate the environmental
 Practices                     footprint of global supply chains→ LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment): India’s global mission to
                               nudge individuals toward sustainable consumption.
 Protection of Vulnerable      Implement measures to shield sensitive sectors like agriculture from the adverse effects of
 Sectors                       globalisation, ensuring inclusive economic benefits.
 Innovation and                Align vocational training with Industry 4.0 demands to empower MSMEs and youth in the
 Entrepreneurship              global digital economy.
 Inclusive Growth and          Strengthen Social Safety Nets and ensure universal access to quality healthcare and education to
 Poverty Alleviation           bridge the "globalization gap."
In conclusion, globalization has interconnected economies and societies, offering vast opportunities. However, to be truly inclusive
and sustainable, it must evolve into “globalization with a human face,” prioritizing equity, ethics, and human well-being.

chapters.text:
8              Communalism in India
Communalism is an ideology that promotes political, social, or emotional mobilisation around religious identity, often creating
hostility between communities. In India, it can be exploited for political gain and may result in social division, violence, and threats
to secularism and national integration. Distinct groups are treated as "imagined communities"(Benedict Anderson), which are
artificially constructed to be in conflict, shifting the focus from shared, peaceful coexistence to politically manufactured rivalry.
                                                   UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Discuss the impact of the post-liberal economy on ethnic identity and communalism.
            (2023)
      2.    ‘Communalism arises either due to power struggle or relative deprivation.’ Argue by
            giving suitable illustrations. (2018)
      3.    Distinguish between religiousness/religiosity and communalism, giving one example of
            how the former has transformed into the latter in independent India. (2017)


                                         Forms of Communalism


                                         Stages of Communalism


1. Causes of Communalism in India
    Factors                                                            Description
Historical             Colonial divide-and-rule policies and the Two-Nation Theory entrenched communal divisions, sowing
Factors                seeds of mistrust and intersectional conflict→ Partition of India (1947) triggered communal riots
                       (1946-48).
Political              Vote-bank politics, divisive rhetoric, and appeasement policies exploit religious identities,
Factors                undermining social cohesion and fueling polarisation→ The Abhiram Singh Case (2017) prohibits
                       religion-based campaigning, yet "Casteisation" and "Politicisation of religion" persist.
Socio-Economic         Poverty, unemployment, and relative deprivation create resentment, making marginalised groups
Factors                vulnerable to communal ideologies →Sachar Committee Report empirically documents this socio-
                       economic backwardness, which is often leveraged to fuel narratives of relative deprivation.
Cultural Factors       Cultural dissimilarities and differing notions of nationalism foster mutual mistrust, eroding India’s
                       syncretic ethos→ Cultural polarisation can be intensified by disputes over food, religious conversion,
                       identity markers, and symbols.
Psychological          Fear of cultural erosion and minority ghettoisation breeds insecurity, amplifying communal tensions
Factors                and social alienation→ Forced exile of Kashmiri Pandits (1989-90) due to communal tensions in Jammu
                       and Kashmir.
Algorithmic            Social media platforms use engagement-driven algorithms that prioritize "out-group animosity" &
Polarization in        content triggering anger toward other communities to maximize screen time. This rapid digital
Social Media           radicalization scales local disputes into major crises→ The 2020 Delhi riots highlight how
                       algorithmically amplified hate speech and fake news weaponize communal tensions.

2. Impacts of Communalism in India
  Aspect                                                               Impacts
Social            y Polarisation: Creates divisions, fostering mistrust and hostility between religious groups.
                  y Violence: Leads to riots, mob violence, and loss of lives.
                  y Segregation: Marginalises minorities, encourages ghettoisation & leading to social exclusion.
                  y Impact on Children: Growing up in communal "hotspots" leads to the normalization of violence and the
                    transmission of prejudice to the next generation.
                  y Trust Deficit: Breaks the social capital, making community cooperation impossible.
                  y Human Rights Violations: Communal conflicts often lead to gross human rights violations, including
                    mass killings, sexual violence, etc., that disproportionately affect women
                  y Example: Nuh Violence (2023): Demonstrated how traditional social contracts between communities can
                    snap, leading to multiple deaths, mass displacement, segregation & widespread mistrust.
Political         y Vote Bank Politics: Parties exploit communal identities to secure votes.
                  y Polarisation of Electorate: Divides voters along religious lines.
                  y Governance Paralysis: Diverts focus from development to conflict management.
                  y Subversion of Secularism: Challenges to constitutional secularism(S.R. Bommai Case).
                  y Example: Muzaffarnagar Riots (2013): Political campaigns exploiting Hindu-Muslim tensions in Uttar
                    Pradesh fueled riots, killing 62 and displacing thousands, influencing the 2014 elections.
Economic          y Disruption of Businesses: Riots and curfews halt economic activities.
                  y Loss of Property: Destruction of homes and shops during clashes.
                  y Flight of Capital: Communal instability acts as a deterrent for FDI and domestic investment due to the risk
                    premium.
                  y Unemployment: Marginalised communities face employment barriers.
Cultural          y Erosion of Ganga Jamuna Tehzeeb: Decay of India's syncretic ethos and interfaith traditions.
                  y Propaganda: Spreads stereotypes and distorted narratives.
                  y Threat to Heritage: Destruction of cultural sites during riots.
                  y Suppression of Expression: Fear of backlash limits artistic freedom.
Internal          y Radicalization: Provides fertile ground for Non-State Actors (e.g., ISIS/Kashmiri militants) to recruit frustrated
Security            youth.
                  y Lone Wolf Threats: Digital radicalization and online grievances bypass traditional networks, enabling non-state
                    actors to inspire self-radicalized, unpredictable "lone wolf" attacks that are highly difficult to preempt.
                  y International Image: Negative impact on Soft Power and diplomatic relations with neighbors and global bodies.


3. Government Measures to Address Communalism in India
   Measures                                                        Key Provisions
Legislative           y Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita(Sec 103(2), 191, 196): Defines Mob Lynching (103(2)) and punishes
                        promoting enmity (196).
                      y Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (1967): Targets extremist propaganda threatening national
                        sovereignty.
Administrative        y Rapid Action Force (RAF): Specialized zero-lead-time response for riot control (e.g., Delhi 2020).
                      y Election Commission of India - Model Code of Conduct: Enforces Article 324 by banning
                        religion-based electoral appeals.
Judicial directives   y Poonawalla Case: Mandates state accountability, nodal officers, and fast-track trials for lynching.
by the SC             y S.R. Bommai Case: Establishes Secularism as the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution.
Educational           y National Integration Council: A high-level platform for communal harmony, chaired by the PM.
                      y Value Education (Art 51A): NCERT focuses on "Scientific Temper" and "Common Brotherhood."
Socio- Economic       y PM’s 15-Point Programme: Targeted welfare (PM-VIKAS, Nai Manzil) to reduce Relative
                        Deprivation.
                      y Sachar Committee: Recommended an Equal Opportunity Commission to address structural
                        discrimination.
Digital/Policy        y Articles 25–30: Constitutional bedrock for religious freedom and minority rights.
                      y PIB Fact Check / Cyber Cells: Countering "Digital Communalism" and online misinformation.

4. Challenges in Addressing Communalism in India
  Dimension                                    Challenge                                              Examples
Political             Identity Mobilization: Casteisation of Politics and                Vote Bank Politics: Exploitation of
                      Politicisation of Religion undermine secular values and            issues like CAA-NRC for electoral
                      deepen divides.                                                    gains.
                      Administrative Interference: Political pressure often leads to     Delhi riots (2020): Delayed police
                      a "Delayed or Biased Action" by local authorities to shield        response.
                      specific groups.
Legal /               Lack of Strong Anti-Communal Legislation: No specific law          Communal Violence Bill (2005)
Institutional         against communal violence; BNS provisions are generic and          lapsed due to opposition over federal
                      insufficient for mass violence.                                    overreach concerns.
                      Poor Conviction: Poor investigation and witness protection         Few convictions in the 2002 Gujarat
                      lead to extremely low conviction rates in mass riot cases.         riots.
Social                Inter-generational Prejudice: Deep-rooted communal                 Communal targeting during
                      narratives are passed down, sustaining long-term social            COVID-19
                      divides.
                      Erosion of Social Capital: A deep Trust Deficit and the lack of    Physical segregation in cities like
                      inter-faith dialogue (civic engagement) foster an "Us vs.          Ahmedabad reduces cross-community
                      Them" mentality.                                                   interaction.
Media /               Spread of Misinformation and Hate Speech: Social media             The 2021 Tripura violence was
Technology            rapidly amplifies fake news and inciting content, often            fuelled by viral fake videos on social
                      unchecked.                                                         platforms.
                      TRP-Driven Sensationalism: Partisan media coverage often           Sensationalism of Delhi riots.
                      prioritizes ideological motives or ratings over ethical
                      reporting.
Administrative        Accountability Deficit: Lack of institutional responsibility for   The failure of police "neutrality" as
                      DMs/SPs to act impartially against rioters.                        noted by the Delhi HC in 2020.
Economic              Marginalisation of Minority Communities: Socio-economic            Sachar Committee: Highlights lower
                      exclusion creates alienation and resentment, leading to            Muslim representation in formal jobs
                      communal flashpoints.                                              and housing.


 5. Communalism in India: Recent Trends & Reasons
                                            Recent Trends in Communalism
 y Micro-Riots & Low-Intensity Conflict: Shift from large-scale riots to small, localized, but frequent flares (e.g., Nuh 2023)
   that keep the social fabric permanently polarized without attracting massive international scrutiny.
 y Spatial Segregation: Increased urban ghettoization where communities retreat into religious enclaves, eroding social
   capital→ Rise of "vegetarian-only" or "religion-specific" housing societies in metropolitan areas.
 y Digital Communalism: Social media acting as a force multiplier for Filter Bubbles and rapid youth radicalization→
   Tripura Violence (2021): Fueled by morphed videos and misinformation spread via messaging apps.
 y Legal-Religious Contestation: Judicialisation of religious disputes has increased communal identity mobilisation at the
   forefront→ Legislative debates on Conversion Laws(Love Jihad cases) and Places of Worship Act in various states (e.g.,
   UP, MP).
 y Manipur Crisis (Ethnic-Communal Overlap): While primarily ethnic (Meitei vs. Kuki), it has taken on communal
   overtones due to the religious identities involved, leading to 220+ deaths and massive internal displacement.

                                               Reasons for the Recent Rise
 y Politicized Ecosystem: Exploitation of religious identities for electoral gain, turning elections into a majoritarian census.
 y Media Partisanship: Bipartisan media and biased academia framing narratives that sustain an "Us vs. Them" mentality→
   Prime-time Debates on communal issues designed to boost TRPs over truth.
 y Institutional Trust Deficit: A perceived breakdown of state neutrality, leading to "Retaliatory Communalism" and
   alienation→ Perceptions of "selective justice" through the use of bulldozers in riot-affected areas.
 y Majoritarian Assertiveness: Rise of religious nationalism, where minority cultural expressions are increasingly
   marginalized→ Politicization of dietary practices & rise of cattle vigilantism, weaponizing cultural differences into street
   level violence.

6. Way Forward for Addressing Communalism in India
     Measures                                                         Description
 Institutional &         y Accountability Framework (2nd ARC): Fix administrative responsibility on the District
 Administrative            Magistrate (DM) and the Superintendent of Police (SP) personally.
 Reforms                 y Equal Opportunity Commission (Sachar Committee): Establishing a dedicated body to address
                           systemic discrimination in housing, employment, etc.
                         y District Nodal Officers(Poonawalla Case): Appointing a senior police officer in every district to
                           monitor and prevent mob lynching and hate crimes.
 Legislative & Legal     y Specific Anti-Hate Speech Laws: Implementing the Law Commission’s 267th Report
 Measures                  recommendations to clearly define and penalize "Hate Speech".
                         y Targeted Violence Legislation: Reviving and enacting the Prevention of Communal and
                           Targeted Violence Bill (2005) to ensure justice and reparations for victims.
                         y Mob Lynching Laws: Following the Supreme Court directive to create a standalone law to provide
                           a deterrent against mob-led communal justice.
                         y Viswanathan Committee: Prevent modern forms of hate crimes by new laws.
 Socio- Economic &       y PM’s 15-Point Programme: Ensuring effective implementation of welfare schemes focusing on
 Educational               education and economic upliftment for minority-concentrated blocks.
 Interventions           y Reservation for Upliftment: Considering the Ranganath Misra Commission recommendation
                           for 15% reservation (10% for Muslims) in education and jobs to bridge the Relative Deprivation
                           gap.
                         y Value-Oriented Pedagogy: Reforming school textbooks to emphasize Article 51A, focusing on
                           the Scientific Temper, humanism, & the spirit of common brotherhood.
                         y Community policing: It prevents communalism by fostering trust, facilitating interfaith dialogue,
                           and addressing local grievances before they escalate into religious violence→ Mohalla Ekta
                           Committees.
To counter communalism, India must uphold "Sarva Dharma Sambhav" (equal respect for all religions) through inclusive policies,
interfaith dialogue, and strict enforcement of secular principles. Only by nurturing unity in diversity can we achieve lasting
peace and progress.

chapters.text:
9                Regionalism
Regionalism refers to a shared sense of identity among people in a specific geographical area based on distinct linguistic, cultural,
ethnic, or economic characteristics, fostering loyalty to the region.
Regionalism in India emerges from diverse linguistic, cultural, and economic identities, often leading to demands for greater autonomy
or separate statehood. In a federal system, it can be a healthy expression of local pride. However, it differs from parochialism—a
narrow, exclusionary mindset that blindly prioritizes local interests and breeds hostility toward outsiders. In contemporary India, this
dynamic is most prominently visible in the intensifying "North-South Divide" narrative. Driven by growing friction over fiscal
devolution, demographic divergence, and the upcoming delimitation of parliamentary seats, this divide has emerged as a significant
structural challenge to federal balance.
While regionalism naturally reflects India’s pluralistic character, it threatens national integration when it hardens into competitive
parochialism. Balanced policies promoting equitable development and cultural preservation are essential to maintain unity amid
diversity.

                                                   UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     What is regional disparity? How does it differ from diversity? How serious is the issue of
             regional disparity in India? (2024)
      2.     Do you agree that regionalism in India appears to be a consequence of rising cultural
             assertiveness? Argue. (2020)
      3.     What is the basis of regionalism? Is it that unequal distribution of benefits of development
             on a regional basis eventually promotes regionalism? Substantiate your answer. (2016)
      4.     Growing feeling of regionalism is an important factor in the generation of demand for a
             separate State. Discuss. (2013)


1. Characteristics of Regionalism in India
   Characteristic                               Description                                              Example
 Historical &              Shaped by colonial legacies (e.g., "Divide and Rule")         Linguistic Reorganization (1956): A
 Structural Legacy         and uneven developmental policies that entrenched             direct response to historical regional
                           regional disparities.                                         consciousness and linguistic pride.
 Collective Regional       Fosters social cohesion through a sense of belonging          Dravidian Movement (Tamil Nadu): An
 Identity                  rooted in linguistic or ethnic traits. It often serves as a   identity rooted in linguistic heritage that
                           platform for subaltern agency against perceived               reshaped regional politics and social
                           central dominance.                                            hierarchy.
 Regional Pride and        Strong attachment to local history can lead to "Sons of       Marathi Manoos (Maharashtra):
 Cultural Hegemony         the Soil" narratives. By rigidly prioritizing native          Mobilization around Marathi identity,
                           identity over migrants, this mindset can lead to structural   which has, at times, led to the
                           discrimination, social stratification, and overt              marginalization of non-native labor.
                           xenophobia targeting those perceived as outsiders.
 Demand for                Regionalism manifests as a quest for governance               Ladakh Statehood Demand: Aspirations
 Autonomy and              autonomy or statehood, usually triggered by perceived         for separate governance to protect fragile
 Self-Determination        structural inequality or administrative neglect by the        ecology and distinct tribal culture.
                           center.
 Diverse Expressions       Regionalism is expressed through diverse lenses—              y Telangana Movement: driven by
 of Identity               linguistic, ethnic, or economic—reflecting India’s              economic neglect
                           deep cultural pluralism.                                      y Bodoland (Assam): focuses on distinct
                                                                                           ethnic survival.


2. Forms of Regionalism in India
     Form                                   Description                                                Example
Secessionism         The most extreme form involves militant or                       Khalistan Movement: Separatist
                     fundamentalist mobilization to withdraw from the Union           movement seeking to create a homeland
                     and form an independent sovereign state.                         for Sikhs.
Separatism           Demand for distinct recognition or partial autonomy (new         Creation of Bodoland for the Bodo-
                     statehood) within the Indian Union, driven by ethnic or          speakers in Assam
                     linguistic distinctions.
Supra-State          Multiple states forming a collective front based on shared       South Indian states are protesting against
Regionalism          socio-historical interests or cultural pluralism to challenge    the upcoming parliamentary delimitation
                     central policies.                                                exercise.
Inter-State          Conflicts between two or more states over shared resources       Kaveri Water Dispute: Resource-based
Regionalism          or territorial boundaries often fuel competitive                 conflict between Karnataka and Tamil
                     regionalism.                                                     Nadu.
Intra-State          Emerges from regional disparities within a state; it             Gorkhaland for ethnic Gorkha (Nepali)
Regionalism          involves parts of a state desiring a separate identity or        people in West Bengal
                     developmental autonomy.
Linguistic           Driven by the protection and promotion of regional               Tamil Nadu’s long-standing opposition to
Regionalism          languages, sometimes opposing the imposition of other            Hindi imposition and the three-language
                     languages.                                                       formula
Cultural             Arises from the desire to protect and promote a region’s         Chauvinistic language mandates as in
Regionalism          unique cultural, linguistic, or historical identity.             signboard controversies.

                                 Son of the Soil (Nativist) Movement: An Analysis
The Sons of the Soil movement in India is a regionalist ideology asserting that the original inhabitants (natives) of a state or
region have a primary right to its resources, employment, and political power over migrants.

                                 Reasons for the Rise of Son of the Soil Movements
y Cultural Prejudice & Identity Preservation: Stronger opposition arises when immigrant populations are ethnically or
  culturally dissimilar→The Assam Movement (1979-1985), which focused on protecting indigenous cultural and political
  rights against illegal migration.
y Economic Competition & Job Scarcity: The Indian economic model has struggled to create sufficient employment,
  leading to intense competition for limited private and public sector jobs.
y Relative Deprivation The rising local middle class often feels bypassed by "outsiders" in terms of education and high-skill
  employment→ IT Sector Hiring Debates in Bengaluru, where local groups express resentment over being sidelined in the
  high-tech labor market
y Political Mobilization: Politicians consolidate their voting base by appealing to populist sentiments and portraying
  themselves as champions of local interests→ Shiv Sena’s early mobilization in Mumbai prioritized Marathi speakers for
  clerical and industrial jobs.
y Historical Redressal: Measures are often seen as a way to address historical injustices or marginalization faced by
  indigenous populations.
y Protectionism: Policies aim to shield local workers from external competition amidst globalization→ Haryana’s local
  employment reservation policy in the private sector


 Positive Impacts of Nativist Policies                           Negative Impacts of Nativist Policies
y Localized Welfare: Prioritising locals in      y Economic Stagnation & National Market Fragmentation: Mandatory
  jobs helps reduce unemployment and               local quotas deter investment and reduce corporate productivity, directly
  ensures livelihood security for native           fragmenting the domestic economy. They undermine the "One Nation,
  populations.                                     One Market" ideal championed by reforms like the GST, treating internal
y Preservation of Regional Culture:                states like separate economic territories.
  Promotes local language, traditions, and       y Rise in Regionalism: Creates tensions between locals and migrants,

  pride and social cohesion.                       North Indian migrants in Maharashtra.

  disparities, pushing governments to invest       weaken unity, promoting divisive politics and undermining national
  in backward areas and reduce inequalities.       cohesion.
y Political Empowerment: Enhances the            y Unsustainable Solution: Reservations provide temporary relief but fail to
  representation of indigenous groups in           address the root cause: the overall lack of macro-economic employment
  governance and decision-making processes.        growth.
                                                 y Restriction on Labour Mobility: Discourages migration, reducing
                                                   workforce flexibility, and affecting sectors dependent on migrant labour.

y Example: The Bodoland movement in Assam seeks                   cultural assertiveness.

  cultural pride can escalate into separatist or                  Failure: Regional leaders often exploit corruption,

  overshadows national unity, reinforcing regionalism             gains. These structural governance failures frequently
  as a cultural phenomenon.                                       masquerade as "cultural" demands because identity is a far
y Example: The Khalistan movement in Punjab                       easier emotional tool for mass mobilization than complex
  leveraged Sikh cultural identity to demand a separate           policy issues.
  homeland, reflecting cultural assertiveness.                  y Example: Regional parties in Punjab capitalize on
                                                                  agricultural policy crises, weaponizing systemic

7. Government Measures to Tackle Regionalism in India
    Theme                                                          Key Measures
Structural &          y Asymmetric Federalism: Accommodates unique historical/ethnic identities via Article 371 (A-J) to
Constitutional          prevent alienation.
Framework             y Territorial Reorganisation: Creation of states based on linguistic/administrative needs
                        (e.g., Telangana, Jharkhand) to bring governance closer to the people.
                      y Local Empowerment: 73rd & 74th Amendments decentralise power to Panchayats/ULBs, addressing
                        local grievances.
Economic &            y Aspirational Districts Programme: Targets 112 lagging districts using data-driven governance to
Developmental           bridge the Inter-regional gap.
Interventions         y Fiscal Federalism: Finance Commission (Art. 280) uses Horizontal Devolution to transfer more
                        funds to poorer states.
                      y Special Infrastructure Schemes: Targeted funding for sensitive zones like NESIDS (North East
                        Special Infrastructure Development Scheme) and Border Area Development Programme.
Institutional &       y Inter-State & Zonal Councils: Constitutional (Art. 263) and Statutory bodies to resolve disputes and
Collaborative           foster Cooperative Federalism.

                        communalism and extreme regionalism.
Socio-Cultural        y Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat: Pairs diverse states (e.g., HR with TS) for cultural exchange to build a
Integration             "Pan-India" identity.
                      y Linguistic Pluralism: 8th Schedule (22 languages) ensures that regional languages are protected,
                        preventing "Linguistic Imperialism" fears.


8. Way Forward for Addressing Regionalism in India

 Economic             Targeted Developmentalism: Shift from State-level         Aspirational Blocks Programme (ABP);
 Convergence          to Block-level planning to eliminate pockets of           Utilizing PM Gati Shakti to integrate backward
                      neglect.                                                  regions into the national supply chain.

 Administrative       Centre-State binary to include Inter-State and Local      263); Empowering Zonal Councils for regional
                      cooperation.                                              dispute resolution.
                      Deepening Decentralization: Ensuring that regional        Participatory Gram Sabhas (e.g., Mandya
                      aspirations are met at the third tier of                  model); Increasing local bodies' share in mining/
                      governance(Principle of Subsidiarity)                     mineral earnings.
 Socio-Cultural       Promoting Constitutional Patriotism: Fostering a          y Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat; Kashi-Tamil
 & Educational        national identity that encompasses, rather than             Sangamam;
                      replaces, regional identity.                              y Implementing the Bezbaruah Committee
                                                                                  recommendations on education.

                      approach to language.                                     promoting handbooks in 22 Scheduled languages.
 Security &           Democratic Accommodation: Prevent violent                 Peaceful formation of Telangana; Engaging
 Grievance            expressions of regionalism through accommodation          local leaders in de-radicalization and peace
 Redressal            and institutional grievance redressal.                    accords in the North-East.
 Migration &          Institutional Integration: Mitigate "Sons of the          Establishing Inter-State Migration Councils to
 Social               Soil" nativist friction by formally integrating and       streamline labor mapping, portable welfare access,
 Integration          protecting interstate migrant workforces.                 and cultural integration programs in host states.
Regionalism in India reflects the vibrancy of its diversity, but must be harmonised through inclusive development, cooperative
federalism, and cultural integration. Embracing regional identities while fostering national unity is key to realising the vision of
Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat, and NARA(National Ambition + Regional Aspirations).
